workflow.title: Clarify
workflow.body: Turn an unclear ticket into a bounded problem and acceptance criteria.

workflow.title: Research
workflow.body: Map the repository, existing patterns, risks and reusable code.

workflow.title: Build
workflow.body: Implement the smallest complete slice with agent-assisted iteration.

workflow.title: Review
workflow.body: Inspect the diff, challenge assumptions and trace risky behavior.

workflow.title: Verify
workflow.body: Run focused tests, browser checks and the production build.

workflow.title: Deliver
workflow.body: Leave a clear change with evidence, rationale and a usable result.

caseStudies.title: Veldia — shift management SaaS and PWA
caseStudies.description: A mobile-first product for small shift-based businesses, covering schedules, hours, availability, roles, onboarding, pricing, trial and manager-reviewed AI schedule drafting.
caseStudies.context: Small shift-based businesses often coordinate schedules and work hours through paper, chat messages and fragmented spreadsheets. Veldia turns those recurring operational tasks into one mobile-first workflow for managers and employees.
caseStudies.roleAndBoundaries: I own the product direction and work across positioning, interface design, React implementation, schedule and hour workflows, onboarding, pricing, trial, SEO and the AI-assisted schedule draft. The case study describes the product surfaces I built and does not claim customer adoption figures that have not been publicly measured.
constraints: The main workflows must remain usable on a phone during or immediately after a shift.
Small-business users need a simple interface rather than enterprise workforce-management complexity.
An AI-generated schedule must remain a draft reviewed and approved by a manager.
The product must support a useful trial and a clear path from onboarding to recurring use.
implementation: React and TypeScript power the application interface and workflow logic.
Supabase provides the backend-connected data and account layer.
Cloudflare serves the product and public acquisition surfaces.
The operational model covers schedules, recorded hours, availability, roles and manager review.
decisions: Treat AI as a drafting assistant, not an autonomous scheduling authority.
Prioritize the recurring hours-and-schedule workflow before adding secondary features.
Design mobile-first because the product is used around shift work, not only at a desk.
aiWorkflow: The product uses AI to propose a schedule draft from the available planning context.
A manager reviews and changes the draft before it becomes the working schedule.
Coding agents are used during development with repository research, review, tests and browser verification.
verification: Core flows are checked in the browser against the same mobile-first paths used by the live product.
Production builds and deployment checks are part of the delivery workflow.
The public product and real interface screenshots provide externally checkable evidence that the system exists.
caseStudies.outcome: A live mobile-first SaaS/PWA with real operational workflows rather than a portfolio-only prototype.
caseStudies.measurement: No public adoption or time-saved metric is claimed. The verifiable outcome is a publicly accessible product with implemented schedule, hour, availability, role, onboarding and trial workflows.
caseStudies.limitations: The case study does not publish private user data, customer-specific usage figures or internal account information.

caseStudies.title: llmpolska — practical AI platform for Poland
caseStudies.description: A Polish community, tools and education platform focused on practical AI work, product discovery and useful implementation knowledge.
caseStudies.context: Polish AI practitioners need a focused place for practical tools, education and community rather than another generic AI news site. llmpolska is structured around useful discovery and implementation-oriented content.
caseStudies.roleAndBoundaries: I work across product positioning, brand, information architecture, navigation, React Router implementation, Cloudflare delivery and AI-tool discovery surfaces. Public claims are limited to the live product and the implementation visible on the site.
constraints: The platform must be understandable to Polish users with different levels of AI experience.
Content and tool pages need crawlable server-rendered output for discovery and sharing.
Brand, navigation and distribution need to work as one product rather than separate marketing exercises.
implementation: React Router provides route-level rendering and application structure.
Cloudflare Workers deliver server-rendered pages close to users.
TypeScript keeps product and content surfaces consistent.
Navigation and discovery surfaces connect community, tools and education.
decisions: Use server rendering so useful pages are available without waiting for client JavaScript.
Organize the platform around practical tasks and tools instead of a chronological news feed.
Treat positioning and distribution as part of the product architecture.
aiWorkflow: The platform includes AI-helper and tool-discovery concepts where they make navigation or implementation knowledge easier to use.
Coding agents support repository research and implementation, followed by normal review and verification.
verification: Server-rendered routes can be inspected directly in the page response.
The production site provides externally accessible evidence of the product, brand and navigation.
Deployment and browser checks are part of the release workflow.
caseStudies.outcome: A live server-rendered platform that combines engineering, distribution and product positioning.
caseStudies.measurement: No traffic or community-size metric is claimed in this case study. The externally verifiable evidence is the live server-rendered platform and its public discovery surfaces.
caseStudies.limitations: The platform is evolving; the case study does not present projected community growth as an achieved result.

caseStudies.title: DOVISTA — SAP reporting automation with UiPath
caseStudies.description: An SAP-related reporting workflow mapped, implemented and deployed with UiPath, Document Understanding and OCR.
caseStudies.context: A purchasing-report workflow required repetitive manual processing and exposed the team to avoidable data-entry errors. The work connected purchasing documents, OCR, validated SAP data and an automated report-generation process.
caseStudies.roleAndBoundaries: During an RPA internship at DOVISTA Polska, I mapped the process with stakeholders, built and deployed the UiPath workflow, integrated the SAP-related steps, added Document Understanding and OCR, and prepared management-facing adoption material. Business data, exact SAP screens and internal timings remain confidential.
constraints: The workflow operated around an existing enterprise SAP process rather than a greenfield system.
Purchasing documents required extraction and validation before automation could continue safely.
The result needed stakeholder confidence, a management demonstration and a deployable operating process.
Confidential business data and internal system details cannot be published.
implementation: Purchasing documents entered a Document Understanding and OCR step.
Extracted values were checked against the validated SAP-related source data.
UiPath orchestrated the repetitive workflow and generated the resulting report.
The deployment included process mapping, stakeholder feedback and adoption material.
decisions: Keep source-data validation in the workflow instead of automating unverified OCR output.
Treat adoption and management communication as part of deployment, not as post-project documentation.
Measure the scoped reporting process rather than presenting a generic automation-performance claim.
aiWorkflow: This was an RPA and document-processing project, not a generative-AI project.
Document Understanding and OCR were used for extraction; business validation remained part of the workflow.
verification: The workflow was reviewed with stakeholders from process mapping through deployment.
The completed process was demonstrated to management.
The result was recorded against the scoped report-generation workflow after deployment.
caseStudies.outcome: Report generation time for the scoped workflow was reduced by 40% while manual data-entry risk was lowered.
caseStudies.measurement: The portfolio records the result as a 40% reduction for the scoped report-generation process. The underlying internal timings, run count and business documents are not public, so the claim is intentionally limited to that workflow.
caseStudies.limitations: No confidential purchasing data, SAP screens, customer information or internal measurement records are published.
